# Figure description: скв_387.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/raw_graphics/Колонки_ скважин/скв_387.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite geophysical well log from a drilled borehole. It appears to be a multi-curve wireline log displayed on a continuous vertical strip, typical of Soviet-era borehole geophysical documentation. The log contains multiple tracks with continuous curve traces plotted against depth.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log appears to cover a single borehole, likely designated in the header as **скв. (скважина) №** followed by a number — the title area at the top references **"74-К ОКСОМОВСКИЙ"** or similar (partially legible), suggesting this is the **Oksomovsky** (Оксомовский) survey area or borehole locality in northern Kazakhstan. The vertical extent of the log appears to cover a substantial depth interval, potentially several hundred metres based on the length of the strip. The bottom of the log is annotated with **"КОНТРОЛЬНОЕ КОПИЕ"** (control copy), confirming this is an archival duplicate of the original log.

---

## Key Content

The log contains **two or three parallel geophysical curves** running vertically down the strip, plotted against a depth scale on the left margin. Key observations include:

- **Left track**: Contains lithological or stratigraphic symbols/annotations in columns, likely recording core descriptions, rock types, or formation boundaries at corresponding depths. Several horizontal annotation lines mark apparent stratigraphic boundaries or casing points.
- **Central track(s)**: Show continuous oscillating curves — these are likely **electrical resistivity (КС — кажущееся сопротивление)** and/or **spontaneous potential (ПС — потенциал самопроизведения)** curves, which are the most common Soviet-era log types. The curves show significant amplitude variation throughout the section, indicating alternating beds of contrasting lithology (e.g., sandstones vs. clays, or mineralised vs. unmineralised intervals).
- **Right track**: Contains a secondary curve or gamma-ray/radioactivity log, showing its own characteristic pattern.
- Notable **high-amplitude excursions** of the central curve(s) appear at several depth intervals, suggesting beds with anomalously high resistivity — potentially indicative of dense, cemented, or mineralised horizons.
- Two annotated zones near the upper-middle portion of the log are labelled, possibly marking **"Горизонт"** (horizon) designations.
- The lower portion of the log (below approximately two-thirds depth) shows more irregular, high-frequency oscillations, suggesting a more heterogeneous or fractured lithological sequence.
- At the very bottom, there is a separate short panel labelled **"КОНТРОЛЬНОЕ КОПИЕ"** with a brief legend box.
- Depth tick marks and grid lines are present throughout, allowing approximate depth reading.
- The right-hand side of the sheet contains a **data table** with multiple columns — likely recording interval-by-interval interpretations, formation tops, or computed parameters.

---

## Text and Labels

Legible and partially legible text includes:

- **Header (top centre):** **"74-К ОКСОМОВСКИЙ"** — borehole or survey designation
- Subheading text (partially legible): appears to include survey organisation name, year, and possibly sheet/area designations
- Mid-log annotations: **"ГОРИЗОНТ №..."** (Horizon No. ...) — marking key stratigraphic levels
- Curve labels (partially legible near top of tracks): likely **"КС"**, **"ПС"**, and possibly **"ГК"** (гамма-каротаж / gamma log)
- Bottom panel: **"КОНТРОЛЬНОЕ КОПИЕ"** — "Control Copy" (i.e., archival duplicate)
- Right-side table headers: column headers for depth intervals and interpreted parameters (specific values not legible at this resolution)
- Left margin: depth scale in metres with regular interval tick marks
- Various handwritten or typed annotations at formation boundaries throughout

---

## Research Value

This borehole log provides a continuous record of subsurface lithological and petrophysical variation with depth at the Oksomovsky locality, allowing geologists to identify formation boundaries, anomalous intervals (high resistivity, radioactivity), and potential mineralised horizons relevant to the regional mineral potential assessment. The log directly supports correlation with other boreholes and geophysical profiles from the M-42/N-42 survey area, enabling construction of a 3D subsurface model. For an AI research assistant, this figure answers the question: *"What does the subsurface look like at this specific drill location, and which depth intervals are geophysically anomalous or potentially mineralised?"*
